title: Medici: Godfathers of the Renaissance . Florence . Palazzo Vecchio
highlights: Grand Duke Cosimo I moved his family to the Palazzo Pitti, their former home was dubbed the "old palace". Its huge bell tower dominates the skyline of Florence. The bell - the vacca (cow) - was melted down by the tyrannical Alessandro de'Medici in the 1530s. With its maze of corridors, council chambers and dungeons, Palazzo Vecchio was the scene of the imprisonment and expulsion of Cosimo il Vecchio, the siege of the Albizzi, the unveiling of Savonarola's theocratic regime, the destruction of Michelangelo's “David”, and the hurried election of Cosimo I as Duke of Florence. title: The Many Names of the Old Palazzo - Gold Colored Fox
highlights: Palazzo della Signoria, named as it was originally after the Signoria of Florence, the city’s ruling body, serves as its town hall. Back when Florence was a republic, it was also known as the Palazzo del Popolo and Palazzo dei Priori, and had its name change to Palazzo Ducale when the city turned into a Duchy. It was only after the dukes of Florence moved their residence to the Palazzo Pitti that the building acquired the name by which it’s most  ...    Built by Arnolfo di Cambio in the beginning of the fourteenth century, the palazzo was constructed upon the ruins of two previous structures owned by the Uberti family, which had rebelled against Florence. title: Medici’s Old Palace – Palazzo Vecchio, Firenze, Italia
highlights: called the Palazzo della Signoria, after the Signoria of Florence, the ruling body of the Republic of Florence, it was also given several other names: Palazzo del Popolo, Palazzo dei Priori, and Palazzo Ducale, in accordance with the varying use of the palace during its long history. The building acquired its current name when the Medici duke’s residence was moved across the Arno to the Palazzo Pitti. Duke Cosimo I de’ Medici moved his official seat from the Medici palazzo in via Larga to the Palazzo della Signoria in May 1540, signalling the security of Medici power in Florence.
